---
title: "Lab 3"
subtitle: "Two-Sample Z-test, Power, and A/A Validation for a Real A/B Experiment"
author: "Ihor Miroshnychenko"
institute: Kyiv School of Economics
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# Objective

In **Lab 1** you designed a one-sample test for your own business case
(comparing the observed metric against a fixed historical baseline
$\mu_0$).

In **Lab 2** you went beyond accept/reject and built confidence
intervals, ran an A/A on your one-sample infrastructure, and wrote a
PM-readable memo.

**Lab 3** takes the next, more realistic step: you switch from
"observed vs. historical baseline" to a **proper two-sample A/B test**
--- two cohorts running in parallel, both with their own noise. The
historical baseline becomes a sanity reference, not a parameter of the
test.

You will:

1. Convert your one-sample framing into a proper two-sample A/B design,
   stating the hypotheses in terms of $p_1$ and $p_2$.
2. Wrap your test logic into a reusable `my_z_test()` function for the
   **two-sample** case, with the pooled-vs-unpooled SE distinction
   correctly implemented.
3. Build the confidence interval **for the lift** and compare it with
   the reference implementation.
4. Plan the experiment: compute power, MDE, and the required sample
   size, and contrast the answer with your one-sample plan from Lab 1.
5. Validate the infrastructure with a **two-sample A/A simulation**,
   and (bonus) check for **Sample Ratio Mismatch (SRM)**.
6. Write a stakeholder-ready recommendation memo.

# General Requirements

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Continuity with Labs 1–2

- You must reuse the **same business case** from Labs 1–2 (same product,
  same metric, same baseline $\mu_0$). Inventing a brand-new case is
  not allowed.
- If your Lab 2 case had methodological issues, fix them in
  @sec-recap and explain what you changed.
- Copying another student's case is prohibited.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Report Format

Submit the report in **one** of the following formats:

- **Quarto → PDF** (`.qmd` → `.pdf`)
- **Quarto → HTML** (`.qmd` → `.html`, hosted separately, e.g. on
  [Quarto Pub](https://quartopub.com/), [Posit Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/), etc.)
- **Jupyter Notebook** (`.ipynb`)
- **Google Colab** (share the link with viewing permissions)

The report must contain both code **and** narrative text. Every section
begins with a written explanation, not code.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Restrictions on libraries

You may use `numpy`, `pandas`, `matplotlib`, `scipy.stats.norm`,
`scipy.stats.binom`, `scipy.stats.chi2`,
`statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint`, and
`statsmodels.stats.proportion.confint_proportions_2indep`.

You **may not** use `statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportions_ztest` or
`scipy.stats.fisher_exact` as the **primary** test --- only as a
**verification step**. The core machinery must be your own code.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Deadline

📅 **June 10, 2026, 23:59**
:::

# Tasks

## Recap of the Lab 2 Case {#sec-recap}

::: {.border}
1. In one paragraph, restate the business problem, the success metric,
   and the historical baseline $\mu_0$.
2. State the **business MDE** with its monetary justification.
3. Briefly summarise what you found in Lab 2: the planned $N$, the
   observed $q$, the one-sample $p$-value, the chosen confidence
   interval, and your decision.
4. If you discovered a methodological issue, state the fix here and use
   the corrected setup throughout Lab 3.
:::

## From One Sample to Two: Design the A/B {#sec-design}

In a real A/B experiment we **do not compare against a frozen
historical** $\mu_0$. We split incoming users into two cohorts ---
**control** (current product) and **test** (new variant) --- and we
test whether their **true conversion rates differ**.

::: {.border}
1. Define the two cohorts in product terms (who sees what, how users are
   assigned, what the randomisation unit is). Make it specific to your
   product.
2. Restate the hypotheses in two-sample form:
   $$H_0: p_1 = p_2 \quad\text{vs.}\quad H_1: p_1 \, \square \, p_2,$$
   where $\square$ is $<$, $>$, or $\neq$. **Choose the alternative**
   and justify it in one sentence (e.g. "we only want to ship if the
   new variant is better, so one-sided 'less' for $p_1 < p_2$").
3. State the **same** $\alpha$ and business MDE you used in Lab 2.
   The MDE is now the smallest interesting **difference** $|p_2 - p_1|$,
   not a deviation from a fixed baseline.
4. Briefly explain (one paragraph) why the historical $\mu_0$ is no
   longer a parameter of the test. What role does it still play in
   *planning* the experiment?
:::

## A Reusable `my_z_test()` Function {#sec-myztest}

::: {.border}
1. Implement a function
   `my_z_test(x1, n1, x2, n2, alternative, alpha, conf_int)` with the
   following contract:
   - Convention: $Z = (\hat p_1 - \hat p_2) / SE_{\text{pool}}$;
     `alternative` is about $p_1 - p_2$.
   - Supports `alternative` $\in$ `{"greater", "less", "two-sided"}`.
   - Uses the **pooled** standard error
     $SE_{\text{pool}} = \sqrt{\hat p_{\text{pool}}(1-\hat p_{\text{pool}})(1/n_1 + 1/n_2)}$
     for the $p$-value.
   - When `conf_int=True`, also returns a CI for the lift
     $p_1 - p_2$ built with the **unpooled** SE
     $SE_{\text{unp}} = \sqrt{\hat p_1(1-\hat p_1)/n_1 + \hat p_2(1-\hat p_2)/n_2}$.
   - Returns a **dict** with at least: `p1`, `p2`, `lift`, `z_stat`,
     `p_value`, `reject`, `ci_low`, `ci_high`.
2. **Explain in one paragraph why the two SEs are different** ---
   when each one is the "right" SE, and what bug would result from
   using `SE_pool` for the CI (or `SE_unp` for the test).
3. Apply the function to a small synthetic A/B based on your business
   case (you may invent realistic $x_1, n_1, x_2, n_2$ at this point;
   the planned $N$ comes in @sec-power). Print the full result.
4. **Verify your implementation** against
   `statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportions_ztest` --- this is the
   only legitimate use of the built-in function. Show that the
   $Z$-statistic and $p$-value match to at least 4 decimal places.
   Be careful with the `alternative` mapping: `statsmodels`'s
   `"smaller"`/`"larger"` is in terms of `prop1 - prop2`.
:::

## Confidence Interval for the Lift {#sec-ci-lift}

::: {.border}
1. For your synthetic A/B from @sec-myztest, compute a $95\%$ CI for
   the lift $p_2 - p_1$ using **three** different constructions:
   - **Wald (unpooled)** via your own `my_z_test`.
   - The reference implementation
     `confint_proportions_2indep(x2, n2, x1, n1, method="wald")` (note
     the argument order: this function reports a CI for $p_2 - p_1$).
   - One **better** method via `confint_proportions_2indep(method=...)`
     (your choice: `"newcomb"`, `"agresti-caffo"`, or `"score"`). Briefly
     state, in one sentence, why this method exists --- what does it
     fix relative to plain Wald?
2. Put all three intervals into a table with columns
   `method | lower | upper | width`. Comment on:
   - Which interval is the **narrowest**, and why is that not a
     decisive reason to prefer it?
   - For which kinds of observed $(\hat p_1, \hat p_2)$ would the
     unpooled Wald interval cross zero in a way the better methods
     would not?
3. **Two business decisions read from the same CI.** Translate the CI
   you computed into product language. Specifically, given the business
   MDE from @sec-design:
   - If the CI for the lift is **entirely above** the MDE → ship.
   - If the CI **straddles zero** → no evidence of a difference.
   - If the CI is **entirely above zero but its lower bound is below
     the MDE** → there is a real lift, but it may not be worth
     shipping. State exactly what you would do in this case for your
     product.
:::

## Power and Sample-Size Planning {#sec-power}

::: {.border}
1. Implement (or reuse from Practice 3) a function
   `my_two_sample_z_power(p1, p2, n1, n2, alpha, alternative)` based on
   the formula:
   $$
   \text{Power}(\text{less}) =
   \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{-z_{1-\alpha} \cdot SE_{\text{null}} - (p_1 - p_2)}{SE_{\text{alt}}}\right),
   $$
   with the symmetric expression for `"greater"` and the two-tailed sum
   for `"two-sided"`. $SE_{\text{null}}$ uses
   $\bar p = (p_1 + p_2)/2$; $SE_{\text{alt}}$ uses the actual
   $p_1, p_2$.
2. **Power vs. $n$ curve.** Plot power as a function of $n_1 = n_2 = n$
   for $n$ from a small value up to a value where power is clearly above
   $0.9$. Use $p_1 = \mu_0$ (your baseline) and
   $p_2 = \mu_0 + \text{business MDE}$. Mark on the plot:
   - The horizontal line at power $= 0.8$.
   - The minimum $n$ at which the curve crosses it.
3. **Plan the experiment.** Compute the required $N$ per group via
   bisection. Compare with your Lab 1/Lab 2 plan:
   - How many users do you need now per group?
   - How does that compare to your one-sample plan (which only needed
     one group of $N_{\text{plan}}$)? Two-sample roughly doubles the
     total user requirement --- but the math is more subtle than just
     "$\times 2$". Explain in one paragraph why
     `(two-sample N per group)` is **not** simply equal to
     `(one-sample N from Lab 1)`.
4. **MDE vs. $n$ trade-off.** Hold power at $0.8$ and produce a
   table or plot of the MDE you can reliably detect as a function of
   $n$ (per group). Pick three or four $n$ values that are realistic
   for your product (e.g. one week / one month / one quarter of
   traffic). Translate each MDE into product language: "with one week
   of traffic we can only detect lifts of at least X pp, which means we
   are blind to anything smaller, which means …"
:::

## A/A Simulation (Two-Sample) {#sec-aa}

::: {.border}
1. Run **$\geq 5\,000$** simulated two-sample A/A experiments under
   $H_0$ with your planned $N$ per group and $p_1 = p_2 = \mu_0$.
   For each one, compute the $p$-value using your `my_z_test`.
2. **Plot the histogram of $p$-values.** Comment on its shape:
   - Why is it expected to be approximately uniform on $[0, 1]$?
   - The two-sample Z-statistic is continuous, unlike the binomial ---
     so the steps you saw in Lab 2 should be much smaller. Why?
3. **Report the empirical FPR** (fraction of $p \leq \alpha$). Compute
   a $95\%$ Wilson CI around the empirical FPR via
   `proportion_confint(method="wilson")`. Is the nominal $\alpha$
   inside it? Use this to argue, with simulation evidence, that the
   test is correctly calibrated.
4. **Two product-specific failure modes.** Describe two realistic ways
   your A/A could fail *for your specific product* (not generic "the
   data are wrong"). Examples of the right level of specificity:
   "same-device cookie collisions between groups", "treatment assignment
   leaks via cached static assets", "weekend traffic is exclusively
   routed to group A". For each failure mode, predict what the $p$-value
   histogram would look like and which direction the FPR would move.
:::

## BONUS: Sample Ratio Mismatch (SRM) {#sec-srm}

In real A/B platforms users are supposed to be split between control
and test in a known ratio (most often 50/50, sometimes 90/10).
A **Sample Ratio Mismatch (SRM)** --- where the observed split differs
significantly from the planned one --- is one of the strongest signals
that something is broken in the assignment pipeline, and it
**invalidates the experiment** *before* you even look at the lift.

::: {.border}
1. Implement an SRM check using $\chi^2$ on a single $1 \times 2$ table:
   $$
   \chi^2 = \sum_{i=1,2} \dfrac{(n_i^{\text{obs}} - n_i^{\text{exp}})^2}{n_i^{\text{exp}}},\qquad
   \text{df} = 1.
   $$
   Compute the $p$-value via `scipy.stats.chi2.sf(chi2, df=1)`.
2. Apply your check to:
   - The synthetic A/B from @sec-myztest (it should *pass* if you set
     $n_1 = n_2$).
   - A clearly broken split (e.g. $n_1 = 2100, n_2 = 1900$ when the
     plan was 50/50). Confirm that the check rejects.
3. Run **$\geq 1\,000$** A/A simulations where each user is assigned
   to a group with probability $0.5$. Plot the histogram of SRM
   $p$-values and confirm that the empirical FPR of the SRM check is
   close to $\alpha$.
4. **One concrete failure scenario.** Describe one realistic way your
   product could end up with SRM that has nothing to do with the
   randomiser (e.g. one variant is heavier and times out for slow
   connections, so those users never make it into the data → the
   broken group has fewer users). What would you do in production if
   the SRM check fails: roll back, debug, or both? In what order?
:::

## Final Recommendation {#sec-final}

::: {.border}
Putting all of the above together, write a **single-page recommendation
memo** (≤ 400 words) addressed to a non-technical stakeholder. It must
contain:

1. The observed point estimates for control and test, the **lift**, and
   the $95\%$ CI for the lift (state which construction and why).
2. The decision (**ship / iterate / extend the test**) and the
   **reason**, framed in terms of the CI vs. the business MDE --- not
   just the $p$-value.
3. Two honest caveats:
   - Did the **A/A check pass** cleanly, or are there open questions
     about the infrastructure?
   - Did the **SRM check pass**? If you did not run SRM (bonus), say
     so and recommend running it before any production rollout.

The memo must be readable by someone who has never heard the words
"$p$-value", "pooled SE", or "SRM". You will be graded on whether a
real product manager could act on it without follow-up questions.
:::

# Grading {.unnumbered}

| Criterion | Points |
|---|:---:|
| Correctness of methodology | 3 |
| Interpretation of results | 3 |
| Code quality and organization | 2 |
| **Total** | **8** |

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Grading Details

**Correctness of methodology (3 pts):**

- `my_z_test` implements all three alternatives correctly, uses
  **pooled SE for the test and unpooled SE for the CI**, and matches
  `proportions_ztest` to 4 decimals (1 pt).
- The three CI constructions in @sec-ci-lift are computed correctly
  and the comparison table is consistent with the reference (0.5 pt).
- The power calculation in @sec-power matches at least one independent
  reference (e.g. `statsmodels.stats.proportion.power_proportions_2indep`)
  to 2 decimals, and the planned $N$ via bisection is the correct
  minimum (1 pt).
- A/A simulation produces an empirical FPR whose Wilson CI contains the
  nominal $\alpha$; the SRM check (if attempted) is correctly built and
  validated by its own A/A (0.5 pt).

**Interpretation of results (3 pts):**

- The pooled-vs-unpooled paragraph is correct and self-contained ---
  you can explain *both* which SE to use *and* why.
- The "CI vs. business MDE" decision rule produces a concrete,
  product-specific recommendation, not a slogan.
- A/A failure modes are realistic and product-specific (no generic
  "wrong data").
- The SRM scenario (if attempted) names a specific, plausible product
  failure rather than "the randomiser is bugged".

**Code quality and organization (2 pts):**

- `my_z_test` and `my_two_sample_z_power` are single reusable
  functions used across all subsequent sections (not re-implemented
  inline).
- Plots have axis labels, titles, and legible legends.
- Narrative text precedes every code block, and each section reads as
  a coherent argument rather than a dump of outputs.
:::

# Defense {.unnumbered}

During the defense (5–7 minutes) you will be asked *"What if?"*
questions that probe whether you understand **why** each piece exists,
for example:

- *"Your test uses pooled SE but your CI uses unpooled SE. Why is that
  not inconsistent? What would a single, unified SE buy or break?"*
- *"The PM says: 'I see a 95% CI of $[+0.001, +0.020]$ for the lift,
  and our business MDE is $+0.010$. Just tell me yes or no.' Give your
  one-sentence answer and your one-sentence rationale."*
- *"Your A/A FPR is $0.062$ instead of $0.050$. Is the difference
  statistically meaningful, or is it just simulation noise? Show me
  the Wilson CI on $0.062$ and use it to answer."*
- *"Switch your test from one-sided 'less' to two-sided in your code
  right now. By how much does the lower CI bound for the lift move,
  and does that change your recommendation?"*
- *"Set the test-group $n$ to half of your planned $N$ and re-run. The
  $p$-value is still below $\alpha$, but the CI got wider. Which one
  drives your decision, and why?"*
- *(Bonus)* *"Your SRM check returned $p = 0.001$ on a 50/50 plan. Do
  you read the lift, debug the platform, or both? In what order, and
  why?"*

You may also be asked to **change one parameter live** in your code
(e.g. flip `alternative`, halve $n_2$, double the MDE) and explain the
resulting shift.
